questions.promptTex: \text{What is the geometric meaning of a solution to a system of two linear equations?}
choices: The midpoint of the two lines
The intersection point of the two lines
hints: What's true about a point on both lines?
questions.explanation: A solution must satisfy both equations, which geometrically means it's a point on *both* lines — i.e., where they intersect.

questions.promptTex: \text{If two lines have the same slope but different y-intercepts, the system has:}
questions.explanation: Same slope, different y-intercepts → parallel lines that never meet → **no solution**.

questions.promptTex: \text{If two equations describe the exact same line, the system has:}
hints: Same slope AND same y-intercept means same line.
Every point on the line satisfies both equations.
questions.explanation: If the two equations describe the same line, every point on that line satisfies both — **infinitely many solutions**.

questions.promptTex: \text{Solve graphically:} \\ y = x \\ y = -x + 4. \\ \text{The intersection is:}
questions.explanation: Setting $x = -x + 4$ gives $2x = 4$, so $x = 2$. Then $y = x = 2$. Solution: $(2, 2)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is one weakness of solving systems by graphing?}
choices: It only works for some systems
It can be imprecise for non-integer solutions
It can't tell you if there's no solution
hints: Imagine reading $(2.7, -1.3)$ off a hand-drawn graph.
Integer points are easy; decimals or fractions less so.
questions.explanation: Graphs can be hard to read precisely when the intersection is at non-integer coordinates. Substitution and elimination give exact algebraic answers — that's why we'll learn those next.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two lines have slopes } 2 \text{ and } 2. \text{ The system has:}
hints: Same slopes → either parallel (no solution) or same line (infinite).
Need to check the y-intercepts to decide which.
questions.explanation: Same slope: if y-intercepts differ → parallel, no solution. If y-intercepts same → same line, infinite solutions. Need more info.
steps: Same slope means the lines are either **parallel** (no solution) or **identical** (infinite solutions).
We can't tell which without checking y-intercepts.
**Depends on y-intercepts.**

questions.promptTex: \text{Two lines have slopes } 3 \text{ and } -2. \text{ The system has:}
hints: Different slopes → lines intersect at exactly one point.
questions.explanation: Different slopes → lines must intersect at exactly one point → one solution.
steps: Different slopes ($3 \neq -2$) → the lines are not parallel.
Two non-parallel lines in a plane meet at exactly **one point**.
**Exactly one solution.**
Set the two expressions for $y$ equal: $x + 1 = -x + 5$.

hints: Distinct parallel lines → never intersect → no solution.
questions.explanation: Same slope, different intercepts → parallel and distinct → no intersection → **no solution**.
steps: Both have slope 3 → parallel.
Different y-intercepts ($1$ vs $-4$) → distinct lines.
Distinct parallel lines never intersect → **no solution**.

hints: Multiply first equation by 2: $4x - 2y = 8$. Same as second!
questions.explanation: Second equation is just $2 \times$ first → they're the same line → infinitely many solutions.
steps: Convert each to slope-intercept (or compare directly).
$2x - y = 4 \Rightarrow y = 2x - 4$.
$4x - 2y = 8 \Rightarrow 2y = 4x - 8 \Rightarrow y = 2x - 4$.
Same line → every point on it is a solution → **infinitely many**.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the solution to } y = -x + 6 \text{ and } y = 2x?
steps: Set the two expressions for $y$ equal: $2x = -x + 6$.
Add $x$: $3x = 6 \Rightarrow x = 2$.

questions.promptTex: \text{If two lines coincide (are the same line), the solution set is:}
hints: Same line → every point on it satisfies both equations.
questions.explanation: Coincident lines = same equation. Every point on the line satisfies both → solution = all points on the line (infinitely many).
steps: Coincident lines have the same equation.
Any point that satisfies one equation satisfies the other.
**Solution set:** every point on the line — infinitely many.
